---
author: Brench
date: 2026-06-28
date-modified: 2026-06-29
open-graph:
  title: chapter 3.4-linear-layer
  description: "3.4 Forward and Backward Passes of a Linear Layer"
title: "Chapter 3.4 Forward and Backward Passes of a Linear Layer"
order: 4
---

[![](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/jshn9515/dnnl-notebooks/blob/main/zh/ch3-multi-layer-perceptron/ch3.4-linear-layer.ipynb){fig-align="left"}

The previous sections covered the basic components of an MLP:

1. linear transformations map inputs into new feature spaces;
2. activation functions introduce nonlinearity;
3. softmax cross entropy converts the final logits into a loss and provides the starting point for backpropagation.

This section examines a module that appears repeatedly in an MLP: the **linear layer**. We first locate linear layers in the overall MLP structure. In practice, an activation function usually follows each hidden layer, but that computation is omitted here.

![Figure 3.4.0: MLP model [@zhang2023d2l, fig. 4.1.1]](figures/chapter-3.1-mlp.svg)

Adjacent layers in an MLP are usually fully connected. Each edge corresponds to a learnable weight, and the complete computation from one layer to the next can be written as a linear transformation.

In a two-layer MLP, linear layers appear twice:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= XW_1 + b_1 \\
Y &= HW_2 + b_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Although their input and output dimensions differ, both perform the same operation:

$$
Y = XW + b
$$

The following sections derive the forward and backward passes of a linear layer and implement a reusable `Linear` class in NumPy. The complete MLP implementation later will use this module directly.

```{python}
import math
from collections.abc import Iterable, Iterator
from typing import Any, override

import numpy as np
import numpy.typing as npt
from dnnlpy.models.mlp import Module, Optimizer

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
print('NumPy version:', np.__version__)
```

## 3.4.1 Forward Pass of a Linear Layer

Suppose a batch contains $B$ samples, each with input dimension $D_{\text{in}}$. The input is

$$
X \in \mathbb{R}^{B \times D_{\text{in}}}.
$$

A linear layer has two learnable parameters, the weight $W$ and bias $b$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &\in \mathbb{R}^{D_{\text{in}} \times D_{\text{out}}} \\
b &\in \mathbb{R}^{D_{\text{out}}}.
\end{aligned}
$$

The forward pass is

$$
Y = XW + b.
$$

The output has shape

$$
Y \in \mathbb{R}^{B \times D_{\text{out}}}.
$$

The bias $b$ is broadcast across the batch dimension, so every sample receives the same bias vector:

$$
Y_i = X_i W + b.
$$

The NumPy implementation is:

```{python}
B = 4
D_in = 3
D_out = 2

X = rng.random((B, D_in))
W = rng.random((D_in, D_out))
b = np.zeros(D_out)

Y = X @ W + b

print('X.shape:', X.shape)
print('W.shape:', W.shape)
print('b.shape:', b.shape)
print('Y.shape:', Y.shape)
```

The matrix shapes satisfy

$$
(B, D_{\text{in}}) @ (D_{\text{in}}, D_{\text{out}}) = (B, D_{\text{out}}).
$$

Thus, the linear layer maps each sample from a $D_{\text{in}}$-dimensional space to a $D_{\text{out}}$-dimensional space.

## 3.4.2 Backward Pass of a Linear Layer

During neural network training, the forward pass produces a loss $L$, and the backward pass computes gradients of the loss with respect to the input and parameters.

For the linear layer

$$
Y = XW + b,
$$

the backward pass receives the upstream gradient

$$
G = \frac{\partial L}{\partial Y}.
$$

It has the same shape as $Y$:

$$
G \in \mathbb{R}^{B \times D_{\text{out}}}.
$$

The layer uses this gradient to compute three quantities:

1. the input gradient $\frac{\partial L}{\partial X}$, which is passed to the preceding layer;
2. the weight gradient $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W}$, which is used to update $W$;
3. the bias gradient $\frac{\partial L}{\partial b}$, which is used to update $b$.

Therefore, the linear layer's backward method receives $G$, returns $\frac{\partial L}{\partial X}$, and stores $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W}$ and $\frac{\partial L}{\partial b}$.

### 3.4.2.1 Gradient with Respect to the Input

For a batch, the linear layer is

$$
Y = XW + b,
$$

where

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &\in \mathbb{R}^{B \times D_{\text{in}}} \\
W &\in \mathbb{R}^{D_{\text{in}} \times D_{\text{out}}} \\
b &\in \mathbb{R}^{D_{\text{out}}} \\
Y &\in \mathbb{R}^{B \times D_{\text{out}}}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Component $j$ of output sample $i$ is

$$
Y_{i,j} = \sum_{k=1}^{D_{\text{in}}} X_{i,k} W_{k,j} + b_j.
$$

A fixed input component $X_{i,k}$ appears in every output component for the same sample:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{i,1} &= \cdots + X_{i,k} W_{k,1} + \cdots \\
Y_{i,2} &= \cdots + X_{i,k} W_{k,2} + \cdots \\
&\vdots \\
Y_{i,D_{\text{out}}} &= \cdots + X_{i,k} W_{k,D_{\text{out}}} + \cdots.
\end{aligned}
$$

To determine how $X_{i,k}$ affects the loss, we sum its contribution across all output dimensions for sample $i$:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial X_{i,k}} =
\sum_{j=1}^{D_{\text{out}}}\frac{\partial L}{\partial Y_{i,j}}
\frac{\partial Y_{i,j}}{\partial X_{i,k}} =
\sum_{j=1}^{D_{\text{out}}} G_{i,j} W_{k,j}.
$$

Because

$$
\frac{\partial Y_{i,j}}{\partial X_{i,k}} = W_{k,j},
$$

we obtain

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial X_{i,k}} =
\sum_{j=1}^{D_{\text{out}}} G_{i,j} W_{k,j}.
$$

In matrix form,

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial X} = G W^\top.
$$

The shapes are

$$
(B, D_{\text{out}}) @ (D_{\text{out}}, D_{\text{in}}) = (B, D_{\text{in}}).
$$

The input gradient therefore has the same shape as $X$.

### 3.4.2.2 Gradient with Respect to the Weights

For the weight gradient, begin with the same batch computation:

$$
Y = XW + b.
$$

Component $j$ of output sample $i$ is

$$
Y_{i,j} = \sum_{k=1}^{D_{\text{in}}} X_{i,k} W_{k,j} + b_j.
$$

A fixed weight $W_{k,j}$ appears in component $j$ of every sample's output:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{1,j} &= \cdots + X_{1,k} W_{k,j} + \cdots \\
Y_{2,j} &= \cdots + X_{2,k} W_{k,j} + \cdots \\
&\vdots \\
Y_{B,j} &= \cdots + X_{B,k} W_{k,j} + \cdots.
\end{aligned}
$$

The effect of $W_{k,j}$ on the loss is therefore summed across the batch:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{k,j}} =
\sum_{i=1}^{B}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial Y_{i,j}}
\frac{\partial Y_{i,j}}{\partial W_{k,j}}.
$$

Because

$$
\frac{\partial Y_{i,j}}{\partial W_{k,j}} = X_{i,k},
$$

we obtain

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{k,j}} =
\sum_{i=1}^{B} G_{i,j} X_{i,k}.
$$

Reordering the product gives

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W_{k,j}} =
\sum_{i=1}^{B} X_{i,k} G_{i,j}.
$$

This is exactly the matrix product

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} = X^\top G.
$$

Its shape is

$$
(D_{\text{in}}, B) @ (B, D_{\text{out}}) = (D_{\text{in}}, D_{\text{out}}).
$$

Thus, $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W}$ has the same shape as $W$. A parameter gradient must match the parameter's shape so that the parameter can be updated element by element.

### 3.4.2.3 Gradient with Respect to the Bias

In the forward pass, the bias is added to every sample's output:

$$
Y_i = X_i W + b.
$$

Bias component $b_j$ affects output component $j$ for every sample in the batch. Therefore,

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b_j} =
\sum_{i=1}^{B} \frac{\partial L}{\partial Y_{i,j}}.
$$

In vector form,

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} = \sum_{i=1}^{B} G_i.
$$

The result has shape $(D_{\text{out}})$, matching the shape of $b$.

### 3.4.2.4 Summary of the Backward Pass

For the linear layer

$$
Y = XW + b,
$$

with upstream gradient

$$
G = \frac{\partial L}{\partial Y},
$$

the backward pass is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial X} &= G W^\top \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} &= X^\top G \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} &= \sum_{i=1}^{B} G_i.
\end{aligned}
$$

In NumPy:

```{python}
G = rng.random((B, D_out))

dX = G @ W.T
dW = X.T @ G
db = np.sum(G, axis=0)

print('dX.shape:', dX.shape)
print('dW.shape:', dW.shape)
print('db.shape:', db.shape)
```

Matrix transposes and reduction axes are common sources of errors in backward-pass implementations. Checking the input, parameter, and gradient shapes at each step makes these errors easier to detect.

## 3.4.3 Implementing a Linear Layer in NumPy

Before implementing the linear layer, we define a `Parameter` class to wrap learnable parameters.

This class subclasses NumPy's `ndarray` and adds a `.grad` attribute for storing gradients. It resembles PyTorch's `nn.Parameter` but does not include Autograd. In the following code, it can be treated as a NumPy array with an additional gradient attribute.

```{python}
class Parameter(np.ndarray):
    __array_priority__ = 1000

    grad: np.ndarray | None

    def __new__(cls, data: Any, dtype: npt.DTypeLike = np.float32):
        obj = np.asarray(data, dtype=dtype).view(cls)
        obj.grad = None
        return obj

    def __array_finalize__(self, obj: Any):
        if obj is None:
            return
        self.grad = getattr(obj, 'grad', None)

    def __array_wrap__(self, out_arr: Any, context=None, return_scalar=False):
        return np.asarray(out_arr)

    @property
    def data(self) -> np.ndarray:
        return np.asarray(self)
```

The linear layer class must perform the following operations:

1. initialize parameters $W$ and $b$;
2. compute $XW + b$ in `forward`;
3. save input $X$ for use during the backward pass;
4. compute `dx`, `dW`, and `db` in `backward`.

```{python}
class Linear(Module):
    def __init__(self, in_features: int, out_features: int):
        super().__init__()
        self.in_features = in_features
        self.out_features = out_features

        W = rng.standard_normal((in_features, out_features))
        W = W * math.sqrt(2.0 / in_features)
        b = np.zeros(out_features)

        self.W = Parameter(W)
        self.b = Parameter(b)

    @override
    def forward(self, x: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        self.ctx = x  # save input for backward
        return x @ self.W + self.b

    @override
    def backward(self, grad: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        assert self.ctx is not None, 'Must call forward before backward.'
        x = self.ctx  # recover saved input

        self.W.grad = x.T @ grad  # dW
        self.b.grad = np.sum(grad, axis=0)  # db
        return grad @ self.W.T  # dx

    @override
    def parameters(self) -> Iterator[Parameter]:
        for value in self.__dict__.values():
            if isinstance(value, Parameter):
                yield value
            elif isinstance(value, Module):
                yield from value.parameters()
```

Here, `grad` is the upstream gradient $G$ from the derivation above.

The weight $W$ uses He initialization:

$$
W \sim \mathcal{N}\left(0, \frac{2}{D_{\text{in}}}\right).
$$

He initialization is commonly paired with ReLU to control the scale of signals as they propagate across layers. The initialization theory is outside the scope of this section; here it serves as a more suitable default than an unscaled standard normal distribution.

The following code checks the shapes of the forward output and all gradients:

```{python}
linear = Linear(in_features=3, out_features=2)
x = rng.random((4, 3))
out = linear(x)

dout = rng.random(out.shape)
dx = linear.backward(dout)

print('out.shape:', out.shape)
print('dx.shape:', dx.shape)
print('dW.shape:', linear.W.grad.shape)
print('db.shape:', linear.b.grad.shape)
```

The `Linear` layer now has the basic interface of a neural network module: its forward pass receives an input, its backward pass receives an upstream gradient, and the resulting input gradient is passed to the preceding layer.

## 3.4.4 Parameter Updates

The linear layer's backward method computes gradients but does not update parameters. Parameter updates are usually handled by an optimizer. The simplest optimizer is SGD:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &\leftarrow W - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial W} \\
b &\leftarrow b - \eta \frac{\partial L}{\partial b},
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\eta$ is the learning rate.

In NumPy:

```{python}
class SGD(Optimizer):
    def __init__(self, params: Iterable[Parameter], lr: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__(params)
        self.lr = lr

    @override
    def step(self):
        for p in self.params:
            if p.grad is None:
                continue

            p -= self.lr * p.grad
```

The following code performs one parameter update:

```{python}
optimizer = SGD(linear.parameters(), lr=0.1)

print('Original W:\n', linear.W)
print('Original b:', linear.b)

print('\n', end='')
optimizer.step()

print('Updated W:\n', linear.W)
print('Updated b:', linear.b)
```

The `parameters()` method returns all learnable parameters wrapped by `Parameter`, namely `W` and `b`. If a module contains child modules, `parameters()` recursively yields their parameters as well.

The implementation separates three responsibilities: forward computes outputs, backward computes gradients, and the optimizer updates parameters from those gradients. Although these operations occur consecutively in a training loop, they represent distinct stages of the computation.

## 3.4.5 Summary

This section derived and implemented the forward and backward passes of a linear layer.

The forward pass is

$$
Y = XW + b.
$$

Given the upstream gradient

$$
G = \frac{\partial L}{\partial Y},
$$

the backward pass is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial L}{\partial X} &= G W^\top \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial W} &= X^\top G \\
\frac{\partial L}{\partial b} &= \sum_{i=1}^{B} G_i.
\end{aligned}
$$

In the implementation, the linear layer saves its input during forward because computing $\frac{\partial L}{\partial W}$ during backward requires $X$. More generally, each layer retains the information needed for its backward computation, then combines that information with the upstream gradient to compute its local gradients.

The linear layer, activation functions, and softmax cross entropy now provide the components required for an MLP. The next section combines them into a complete NumPy implementation.
